text: I'm sharing a R-tap for a right direct hernia. Very symptomatic for this gentleman.
Robotic tap has become my more comfortable way of fixing these hernias, although there are certainly many other options.
The surgeon creates a flap in the tissue, preparing the area for further hernia repair steps.
Trying to stay prepared to kneel. Oftentimes you end up pre-transversalis.

When you're above the arcuate line not sure it really makes that much of a difference, but here i'm just saying prepare to Neil and usually doing medial they section first just because it's a little bit easier.
The surgeon begins lateral dissection in the area of the hernia repair, using robotic instruments to carefully separate tissue layers.
The surgeon searches for the cord structures, which begin to come into view.
The surgeon gently sweeps the chord structures downward, noting the absence of an indirect hernia sac in the surgical field.
The surgeon identifies the vas deferens and continues to follow the dissection plane with the peritoneum already retracted.
The surgeon dissects on the medial surface of the direct hernia, creating space before proceeding to reduce the hernia.
Reducing direct hernias tends to be a lot easier than indirect.
The surgeon dissects past the midline to the contralateral side, creating space for mesh placement in the area of the direct hernia.
The surgeon carefully removes residual fat from the vessels and the pubic sidewall, ensuring a flat surface for mesh placement.
The surgeon approaches the operator space, revealing a visible fat plug commonly found in this area during hernia procedures.
The surgeon begins parietalizing the cord structures, separating them from surrounding tissues to facilitate mesh placement.
The surgeon checks for a lipoma and reduces it, ensuring it is positioned back far enough to be left on the parietal surface of the mesh.
The surgeon imbricates the hernia sac using a suture, preparing it for secure closure.
I have seen numerous ways of dealing with this defect, including closure, or tacking of the Pseudosac to the anterior surface of the abdominal wall.

I continue to do it this way for now and very selectively close direct defects. I've been happy with the results and in the end, I'm not sure if one or any of these techniques really makes that much of a difference, but I do like to have some sort of tissue coverage over this hole so that in my mind it might prevent Eventration of the mesh into the defect in the early post-op period.
The surgeon places a 16x12 anatomic mesh more medially to ensure coverage over the direct hernia site. The mesh is positioned to sit flat, covering the obturator and femoral spaces.
The surgeon secures the mesh medially over the direct hernia defect, ensuring it is positioned to prevent potential migration into the defect before healing.
The surgeon identifies a lipoma extending beyond the mesh and proceeds to close the flap over the surgical site.
The surgeon examines the dissected space and places an angiocath into the flap, connecting it to a suction device for drainage.
The surgeon evacuates gas from the surgical site, allowing for inspection to confirm that the mesh is positioned flat and covers the necessary anatomical spaces.
